subject: Physics
q: In the Bohr model an electron moves in a circular orbit around the proton. Considering the orbiting electron to be a circular current loop, the magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom, when the electron is in $n^{\text {th }}$ excited state, is :
question: In the Bohr model an electron moves in a circular orbit around the proton. Considering the orbiting electron to be a circular current loop, the magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom, when the electron is in $n^{\text {th }}$ excited state, is :
solution:
Magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom, when the electron is in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ excited state, i.e., $n^{\prime}=(n+1)$
As magnetic moment $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{n}}\left(\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}^2\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
& i_n=e V_n=\frac{m z^2 e^5}{4 \varepsilon_0^2 n^3 h^3} \\
& r_n=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 k z m e^2}\left(k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\right)
\end{aligned}$
Solving we get magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom for $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ excited state
$M_{n^{\prime}}=\left(\frac{e}{2 m}\right) \frac{n h}{2 \pi}$

subject: Physics
q: In a hydrogen like atom electron makes transition from an energy level with quantum number <math> <mrow> <mtext>n</mtext> </mrow> </math> to another with quantum number <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mtext>n</mtext> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math>. If <math> <mrow> <mtext>n >> 1</mtext> </mrow> </math>, the frequency of radiation emitted is proportional to :
question: In a hydrogen like atom electron makes transition from an energy level with quantum number <math> <mrow> <mtext>n</mtext> </mrow> </math> to another with quantum number <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mtext>n</mtext> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math>. If <math> <mrow> <mtext>n >> 1</mtext> </mrow> </math>, the frequency of radiation emitted is proportional to :
solution:
Δ E = h ν
ν = Δ E h = k 1 n - 1 2 - 1 n 2 = k 2 n n 2 n - 1 2

≈ 2 k n 3 ∝ 1 n 3

subject: Physics
q:
Muon $\left(\mu^{-1}\right)$ is negatively charged $(|\mathrm{q}|=|\mathrm{e}|)$ with a mass $\mathrm{m}_\mu=200 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}$, where $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}$ is the mass of the electron and e is the electronic charge. If $\mu^{-1}$ is bound to a proton to form a hydrogen like atom, identify the correct statements
(A) Radius of the muonic orbit is 200 times smaller than that of the electron
(B) the speed of the $\mu^{-1}$ in the $n$th orbit is $\frac{1}{200}$ times that of the election in the nth orbit
(C) The lonization energy of muonic atom is 200 times more than that of an hydrogen atom
(D) The momentum of the muon in the nth orbit is 200 times more than that of the electron
question:
Muon $\left(\mu^{-1}\right)$ is negatively charged $(|\mathrm{q}|=|\mathrm{e}|)$ with a mass $\mathrm{m}_\mu=200 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}$, where $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}$ is the mass of the electron and e is the electronic charge. If $\mu^{-1}$ is bound to a proton to form a hydrogen like atom, identify the correct statements
(A) Radius of the muonic orbit is 200 times smaller than that of the electron
(B) the speed of the $\mu^{-1}$ in the $n$th orbit is $\frac{1}{200}$ times that of the election in the nth orbit
(C) The lonization energy of muonic atom is 200 times more than that of an hydrogen atom
(D) The momentum of the muon in the nth orbit is 200 times more than that of the electron
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { (A) Radius of muon }=\frac{\text { Radius of hydrogen }}{200} \\
&\text { Radius of } \mathrm{H} \text { atom }=r=\frac{\in_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m e^2} \\
&\text { Radius of muon }=r_\mu=\frac{\in_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi \times 200 m e^2} \\
&r_\mu=\frac{r}{200}
\end{aligned}
$$
(B) Velocity relation given is wrong
(C) Ionization energy in $e^{-} \mathrm{H}$ atom
$$
E=\frac{+m e^4}{8 \epsilon_0^2 n^2 h^2}
$$

$$
E_\mu=\frac{200 m e^4}{8 \epsilon_0^2 n^2 h^2}=200 E
$$
(D) Momentum of $\mathrm{H}$-atom
$$
m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}
$$
Hence (A), (C), (D) are correct.

subject: Physics
q: The frequency of revolution of the electron in Bohr's orbit varies with $n$, the principal quantum number as
question: The frequency of revolution of the electron in Bohr's orbit varies with $n$, the principal quantum number as
solution: $\begin{aligned} & f=\frac{v}{2 \pi r}=\left(\frac{V_0}{n}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi r_0 n^2}\right) \\ & f=\frac{V_0}{2 \pi r_0}=\left(\frac{1}{n^3}\right)=f_0\left(\frac{1}{n^3}\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two electrons are moving in orbits of two hydrogen like atoms with speeds $3 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $2.5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ respectively. If the radii of these orbits are nearly same then the possible order of energy states are $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
question: Two electrons are moving in orbits of two hydrogen like atoms with speeds $3 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $2.5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ respectively. If the radii of these orbits are nearly same then the possible order of energy states are $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
solution:
For hydrogen-like atoms:
$V \propto \frac{Z}{n}$ and $r \propto \frac{n^2}{Z}$
Thus $r \propto \frac{n}{V}$
Since the radii are nearly the same:
$\frac{n_1}{V_1} = \frac{n_2}{V_2}$
$\frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{3 \times 10^5}{2.5 \times 10^5} = \frac{6}{5}$
Possible order of energy states is 6 and 5.

subject: Physics
q: As per given figure A, B and C are the first, second and third excited energy levels of hydrogen atom respectively. If the ratio of the two wavelengthsi.e. λ1λ2 is 74n, then the value of n will be
question: As per given figure A, B and C are the first, second and third excited energy levels of hydrogen atom respectively. If the ratio of the two wavelengthsi.e. λ1λ2 is 74n, then the value of n will be
solution:
For λ1

1λ1=RZ2(122-132)=R536   ...(i)

For λ2,

1λ2=RZ2(132-142)=R7144   ...(ii)

Dividing (ii) by (i)

λ1λ2=7144536=720

So, 

74n=720⇒n=5

subject: Physics
q: A hydrogen atom in its ground state absorbs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> of energy. The angular momentum of electron of the hydrogen atom will increase by the value of (Given, Planck's constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Js</mi></math>).
question: A hydrogen atom in its ground state absorbs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> of energy. The angular momentum of electron of the hydrogen atom will increase by the value of (Given, Planck's constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Js</mi></math>).
solution:
Energy absorbed is given by,

ΔE=13.6×1n12-1n22

10.2=13.6×1n12-1n22

Therefore, we get, n1=1 & n2=2

The change in angular momentum

ΔL=n2h2π-n1h2π=h2π

=1.05×10-34 J s

subject: Physics
q: The mass of proton, neutron and helium nucleus are respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0073</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0087</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0015</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>. The binding energy of helium nucleus is:
question: The mass of proton, neutron and helium nucleus are respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0073</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0087</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0015</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>. The binding energy of helium nucleus is:
solution:
Given here,  mHe=4.0015 u, mp=1.0073 u and mn=1.0087 u.

Mass defect

 ∆m = 2mp+2mn-mHe=2×1.0073+2×1.0087-4.0015=0.0305 u

Binding energy 

E = ∆m×931 MeV=0.0305×931=28.4 MeV

subject: Physics
q: The smallest wavelength of Lyman series is 91 nm. The difference between the largest wavelengths of Paschen and Balmer series is nearly $\_\_\_\_$ nm .
question: The smallest wavelength of Lyman series is 91 nm. The difference between the largest wavelengths of Paschen and Balmer series is nearly $\_\_\_\_$ nm .
solution:
Smallest wavelength of lyman
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R\left(\dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{\infty^2}\right)$
$R = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{91} \text{ nm}^{-1}$
$\lambda_{max}$ for balmer series
$n_1 = 2 \rightarrow n_2 = 3$
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_B} = R\left(\dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9}\right)$
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_B} = \dfrac{1}{91}\left(\dfrac{5}{36}\right)$
$\lambda_B = \left(\dfrac{91 \times 36}{5}\right) = 655.2 \text{ nm}$
$\lambda_{max}$ paschen
$n_1 = 3 \rightarrow n_2 = 4$
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_p} = \dfrac{1}{91}\left(\dfrac{1}{3^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2}\right) = \dfrac{1}{91} \times \dfrac{7}{144}$
$\lambda_p = \left(\dfrac{91 \times 144}{7}\right) = 1872 \text{ nm}$
$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_P - \lambda_B = 1872 - 655.2$
$\Delta\lambda = 1216.8$
$\Delta\lambda \approx 1217$

subject: Physics
q: If one were to apply the Bohr model to a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>'</mo></math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>'</mo></math> moving in a plane under the influence of a magnetic field 'B', the energy of the charged particle in the <math><msup><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> level will be:
question: If one were to apply the Bohr model to a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>'</mo></math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>'</mo></math> moving in a plane under the influence of a magnetic field 'B', the energy of the charged particle in the <math><msup><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> level will be:
solution:
For a charge q moving in a uniform magnetic field B




fm=mv2R 

qVB= mv2R 

⇒           mv2=qVBR

⇒        12 mv2=qVBR2

⇒     Energy=qVBR2      (1)

By Bohr's quantization condition, 

Angular momentum L=nh2π

⇒     mvR=nh2π

⇒     vR=nh2π m       (2)

Put (2) in (1)

⇒     Energy=qB2 nh2 π m

= qB nh4 π m

subject: Physics
q: A neutron moving with a speed 'v' makes a head on collision with a stationary hydrogen atom in ground state. The minimum kinetic energy of the neutron for which  perfactly inelastic collision will take place is :
question: A neutron moving with a speed 'v' makes a head on collision with a stationary hydrogen atom in ground state. The minimum kinetic energy of the neutron for which  perfactly inelastic collision will take place is :
solution:
Let velocity before collision v from momentum conservation mv=( m+m ) v 1

v 1 = v 2

Loss in K.E. =12mv2-122mv22

=14 mv2

K.E. lost is used to jump from 1st orbit to 2nd orbit ∆ K.E.=10.2 eV

⇒  14 mv2=10.2

12 mv2=2×10.2=20.4 eV

subject: Physics
q: Nucleus a having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>17</mn></math> and equal number of protons and neutrons has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> binding energy per nucleon. Another nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> has total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>26</mn></math> nucleons and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> binding energy per nucleons. The difference of binding energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MeV</mi></math>.
question: Nucleus a having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>17</mn></math> and equal number of protons and neutrons has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> binding energy per nucleon. Another nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> has total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>26</mn></math> nucleons and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> binding energy per nucleons. The difference of binding energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MeV</mi></math>.
solution:
In A, Z=17 and have equal numbers of protons and neutrons i.e. nucleons=2×17=34

So, for A, 

Total binding energy BEA=1.2×34=40.8 MeV

Another nucleus B mass number =26

So, total binding energy of  B is BEB=1.8×26 MeV=46.8 MeV

Difference of BE=BEB-BEA=6 MeV

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></msqrt></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></msqrt></math> are the impact parameters corresponding to scattering angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math> respectively, when an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle is approaching a gold nucleus. For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></msqrt></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></msqrt></math> are the impact parameters corresponding to scattering angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math> respectively, when an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> particle is approaching a gold nucleus. For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
solution:
The relation between impact parameter and scattering angle is b=kze2Ecotθ2

⇒b∝cotθ2

⇒d1∝cot60°2

⇒d2∝cot90°2

d1d2=cot60°2cot90°2=3⇒d1=3d2⇒x=3

subject: Physics
q: Angular momentum of an electron in a hydrogen atom is $\dfrac{3h}{\pi}$, then the energy of the electron is _______ eV.
question: Angular momentum of an electron in a hydrogen atom is $\dfrac{3h}{\pi}$, then the energy of the electron is _______ eV.
solution:
The angular momentum of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by Bohr's quantization rule:

$L = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

Given that the angular momentum is $\dfrac{3h}{\pi}$, we can equate the two expressions:

$\dfrac{nh}{2\pi} = \dfrac{3h}{\pi}$

Solving for $n$, we get:

$n = 6$

The energy of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by the formula:

$E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$

Substituting $n = 6$ into the energy formula:

$E_6 = -\dfrac{13.6}{6^2} \text{ eV}$

$E_6 = -\dfrac{13.6}{36} \text{ eV}$

$E_6 \approx -0.377 \text{ eV} \approx -0.38 \text{ eV}$

Answer: $-0.38$

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>917</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math>  be the lowest wavelength of Lyman series then the lowest wavelength of Balmer series will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math>.
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>917</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math>  be the lowest wavelength of Lyman series then the lowest wavelength of Balmer series will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math>.
solution:
Using Rydberg's formula,

1λ=RZ21n21-1n22

For the lowest wavelength in Lyman series, n1=1, n2=∞.

1λ=RZ2112-1∞2=RZ2

For Balmer series, n1=2, n2=∞

1λ'=RZ2122-1∞2=RZ24

So,

 1λ'=14λ⇒λ'=4×917  Å=3668  Å

subject: Physics
q:
xx+4 is the ratio of energies of photons produced due to transition of an electron of hydrogen atom from its

(i) third permitted energy level to the second level and

(ii) the highest permitted energy level to the second permitted level.

The value of x will be
question:
xx+4 is the ratio of energies of photons produced due to transition of an electron of hydrogen atom from its

(i) third permitted energy level to the second level and

(ii) the highest permitted energy level to the second permitted level.

The value of x will be
solution:
Energy of a photon released in transition of electron in hydrogen atom is given by,

E=13.61n22-1n12 eV

⇒E1E2=13.614-1913.614-1∞=xx+4⇒14-1914=xx+4

⇒59=xx+4

⇒5x+20=9x

⇒x=5

subject: Physics
q: Hydrogen atom is bombarded with electrons accelerated through a potential different of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math>, which causes excitation of hydrogen atoms. If the experiment is being formed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The minimum potential difference needed to observe any Balmer series lines in the emission spectra will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.(Write the value to the nearest integer)
question: Hydrogen atom is bombarded with electrons accelerated through a potential different of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math>, which causes excitation of hydrogen atoms. If the experiment is being formed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The minimum potential difference needed to observe any Balmer series lines in the emission spectra will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.(Write the value to the nearest integer)
solution:
For minimum potential difference, electron has to make transition from n=3 to n=2  state but first electron has to reach to n=3 state from ground state. So, energy of bombarding electron should be equal to energy difference of n=3 and n=1 state.

ΔE=13.61-132e=eV

⇒13.6×89=V

⇒V=12.09 V≈12.1 V

So, α=121.

subject: Physics
q: For hydrogen atom, λ1 and λ2 are the wavelengths corresponding to the transitions 1 and 2 respectively as shown in figure. The ratio of λ1 and λ2 is x32. The value of x is ______.
question: For hydrogen atom, λ1 and λ2 are the wavelengths corresponding to the transitions 1 and 2 respectively as shown in figure. The ratio of λ1 and λ2 is x32. The value of x is ______.
solution:
Wavelength corresponding to the photon emitted in transition n=3 to n=1 is

1λ1=R112-132=8R9   ....(1)

Wavelength corresponding to the photon emitted for transition in transition n=2 to n=1 is

1λ2=R112-122=3R4    ....(2)

λ1λ2=3R4×98R=2732

Hence, the value of x=27.

subject: Physics
q: Hydrogen atom is excited from ground state to another state with principal quantum number equal to $4$. Then the number of spectral lines in the emission spectra will be
question: Hydrogen atom is excited from ground state to another state with principal quantum number equal to $4$. Then the number of spectral lines in the emission spectra will be
solution:
Number of spectral lines from a state $\mathrm{n}$ to ground state is
$=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}=6.$

q:
In an alpha particle scattering experiment distance of closest approach for the $\alpha$ particle is $4.5 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}$. If target nucleus has atomic number 80 , then maximum velocity of $\alpha$ - particle is _______ $\times 10^5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ approximately.
$\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9\right.$ SI unit, mass of $\alpha$ particle $\left.=6.72 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}\right)$
question:
In an alpha particle scattering experiment distance of closest approach for the $\alpha$ particle is $4.5 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}$. If target nucleus has atomic number 80 , then maximum velocity of $\alpha$ - particle is _______ $\times 10^5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ approximately.
$\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9\right.$ SI unit, mass of $\alpha$ particle $\left.=6.72 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} v & =\sqrt{\frac{4 \mathrm{KZe}^2}{\mathrm{mr}_{\text {min }}}} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{4 \times 9 \times 10^9 \times 80}{6.72 \times 10^{-27} \times 4.5 \times 10^{-14}}} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \\ & =9.759 \times 10^{25} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \\ & =156 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ion in its first state is, (The ground state energy for the Hydrogen atom<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>):
question: The energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ion in its first state is, (The ground state energy for the Hydrogen atom<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>):
solution:
Energy of Hydrogen like atoms is, E=-13.6Z2n2eV.

Since Helium is in the first state, Z=2, n=2.

The energy of He+ ion is,

E=-13.6×2222=-13.6 eV

q:
The energy levels of an atom is shown is figure.

Which one of these transitions will result in the emission of a photon of wavelength 124.1 nm?

Given (h = 6.62 × 10–34 J s)
question:
The energy levels of an atom is shown is figure.

Which one of these transitions will result in the emission of a photon of wavelength 124.1 nm?

Given (h = 6.62 × 10–34 J s)
solution:
The change in energy is related to wavelength of photon emitted as:

∆E=hcλ=6.62×10-34 J s×3×108 m s-11.6×10-19 J×124.1 nm=1242 eV nm124.1 nm=10 eV

Hence, option D is correct.

q: In a hydrogen atom the electron makes a transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math>  level to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>th</mi></msup></math> level. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mn>1</mn></math>, the frequency of radiation emitted is proportional to :
question: In a hydrogen atom the electron makes a transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math>  level to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>th</mi></msup></math> level. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mn>1</mn></math>, the frequency of radiation emitted is proportional to :
solution:
En=-Rhcn2
En+1=-Rhc(n+1)2
ΔE=En+1-En
hv=Rhc1n2-1(n+1)2
v=R.cn+12-n2n2n+12
v=R.c1+2nn2n+12

n >> 1 ⇒v=R.c2nn2×n2=2RCn3

v∞1n3

q: An electron of hydrogen atom on an excited state is having energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>. question: An electron of hydrogen atom on an excited state is having energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>. The maximum number of allowed transitions to lower energy level is _______.
solution:
Energy of nth orbit is given by, En=-13.6n2

⇒-13.6n2=-0.85⇒n=4

No of transition,

=n(n-1)2=4(4-1)2=6

subject: Physics
q: In hydrogen atom spectrum, $(R \rightarrow$ Rydberg's constant $)$ A. the maximum wavelength of the radiation of Lyman series is $\frac{4}{3 R}$ B. the Balmer series lies in the visible region of the spectrum C. the minimum wavelength of the radiation of Paschen series is $\frac{9}{R}$ D. the minimum wavelength of Lyman series is $\frac{5}{4 R}$ Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question: In hydrogen atom spectrum, $(R \rightarrow$ Rydberg's constant $)$ A. the maximum wavelength of the radiation of Lyman series is $\frac{4}{3 R}$ B. the Balmer series lies in the visible region of the spectrum C. the minimum wavelength of the radiation of Paschen series is $\frac{9}{R}$ D. the minimum wavelength of Lyman series is $\frac{5}{4 R}$ Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
A. Maximum wavelength of Lyman series ($n=1$ to $n=2$):
$\frac{1}{\lambda} = R\left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{3R}{4}$
$\lambda = \frac{4}{3R}$ TRUE

B. The Balmer series lies in the visible region of the spectrum. TRUE

C. Minimum wavelength of Paschen series ($n=3$ to $n=\infty$):
$\frac{1}{\lambda'} = R\left(\frac{1}{9}\right)$
$\lambda' = \frac{9}{R}$ TRUE

D. Minimum wavelength of Lyman series ($n=1$ to $n=\infty$):
$\frac{1}{\lambda} = R\left(1\right)$
$\lambda = \frac{1}{R}$, NOT $\frac{5}{4R}$ FALSE

So A, B and C are correct.

subject: Physics
q: A <math><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> ion is in its first excited state. Its ionization energy is:
question: A <math><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> ion is in its first excited state. Its ionization energy is:
solution:
E=-13.6z2n2
=-13.62222=-13.60 eV
Total energy of He+ ion is in its first excited state is -13.60 eV . Hence, its ionization energy will be 13.60 eV

subject: Physics
q: In a hydrogen spectrum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> be the wavelength of first transition line of Lyman series. The wavelength difference will be "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi>λ</mi></math>" between the wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>rd</mi></msup></math> transition line of Paschen series and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mi>nd</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> transition line of Balmer Series where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>=</mo></math> _____.
question: In a hydrogen spectrum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> be the wavelength of first transition line of Lyman series. The wavelength difference will be "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi>λ</mi></math>" between the wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>rd</mi></msup></math> transition line of Paschen series and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mi>nd</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> transition line of Balmer Series where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>=</mo></math> _____.
solution:
Using Rydberg formula,

1λ=R1nf2-1ni2, where, R is Rydberg constant and n is quantum number.

For first line of Lyman series

1λ=R1-14=R34

⇒λ=43R      ...1

For 3rd  line of Paschen series

1λ3=R132-162=R9×34

For 2nd line of Balmer series

1λ2=R122-142=R4×34

Thus, aλ=λ3-λ2=12R-163R=203R

Putting equation 1

a43R=203R⇒a=5

subject: Physics
q:
The graph which depicts the results of Rutherford gold foil experiment with α -particles is:
θ: Scattering angle
Y: Number of scattered α -particles detected
(Plots are schematic and not to scale)
question:
The graph which depicts the results of Rutherford gold foil experiment with α -particles is:
θ: Scattering angle
Y: Number of scattered α -particles detected
(Plots are schematic and not to scale)
solution:
Rutherford carried out an experiment in which he bombarded a thin sheet of gold foil with α-particles and then analysed the trajectory of these particles after they collided with the gold foil.

For a detector at a specific angle with respect to the incident beam, the number of particles per unit area striking the detector is given by the Rutherford formula,

Yθ=NinLZ2k2e44r2KE2sin4θ2

Here, Ni= Number of incident alpha particles,

n= Atoms per unit volume in target,

L= Thickness of target,

Z= Atomic number of target,

e= Electron charge,

k= Coulomb's constant,

r= Distance between target and detector,

KE= Kinetic energy of alpha particles,

θ= Scattering angle.

Therefore, Y∝1sin4θ2

subject: Physics
q: In a hydrogen like atom, when an electron jumps from the M-shell to the L-shell, the wavelength of emitted radiation is $L$. If an electron jumps from $\mathrm{N}$ -shell to the $\mathrm{L}$ -shell, the wavelength of emitted radiation will be:
question: In a hydrogen like atom, when an electron jumps from the M-shell to the L-shell, the wavelength of emitted radiation is $L$. If an electron jumps from $\mathrm{N}$ -shell to the $\mathrm{L}$ -shell, the wavelength of emitted radiation will be:
solution:
When electron jumps from $\mathrm{M} \rightarrow \mathrm{L}$ shell

$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{K}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{K} \times 5}{36}$

When eletron jumps from $\mathrm{N} \rightarrow \mathrm{L}$ shell

$\frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}=K\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right)=\frac{K \times 3}{16}$

solving equation (i) and (ii) we get

$\lambda^{\prime}=\frac{20}{27} \lambda$

q: The radius of fifth orbit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Li</mi><mrow><mo>+</mo><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Take: radius of hydrogen atom<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>51</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math>
question: The radius of fifth orbit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Li</mi><mrow><mo>+</mo><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Take: radius of hydrogen atom<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>51</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math>
solution:
The formula to calculate the radius of Lithium-ion is given by

RLi=RH×n2Z   ...(1)

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required radius of the ion.

RLi=0.51×253×10-10 m=425×10-12 m

q: The electron in a hydrogen atom first jumps from the third excited state to the second excited state and subsequently to the first excited state. The ratio of the respective wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> of the photons emitted in this process is:
question: The electron in a hydrogen atom first jumps from the third excited state to the second excited state and subsequently to the first excited state. The ratio of the respective wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> of the photons emitted in this process is:
solution:
1λ1=R132-142= R×7144
1λ2=R122-132= R×536
⇒λ1λ2=R×536×144R×7=207

q: A particular hydrogen like ion emits radiation of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> when it makes transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The frequency in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math> of radiation emitted in transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> will be:
question: A particular hydrogen like ion emits radiation of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> when it makes transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The frequency in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math> of radiation emitted in transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> will be:
solution:
As we know,

ν∝1n12−1n22

2.92×1015ν=11−1911−14

ν=2.92×1015×34×98

ν=2.46×1015 Hz

q:
In the given figure, the energy levels of hydrogen atom have been shown along with some transitions marked A,B,C,D and E.
The transitions A,B and C respectively represent
question:
In the given figure, the energy levels of hydrogen atom have been shown along with some transitions marked A,B,C,D and E.
The transitions A,B and C respectively represent
options: The ionization potential of hydrogen, second member of Balmer series and third member of Paschen series.
The series limit of Lyman series, second member of Balmer series and second member of Paschen series.
The series limit of Lyman series, third member of Balmer series and second member of Paschen series.
The first member of the Lyman series, third member of Balmer series and second member of Paschen series.
solution:
A→ Series limit of Lyman series.

B→ Third member of Balmer series.

C→ Second member of Paschen series.
